results.title: Suffering
results.description: Unpleasantness, discontentment, dissatisfaction, stress, pain, disease, i.e. mild or intense suffering that is inherent in conditioned existence.

results.title: Dukkha sutta - Suffering
results.description: Six qualities to dwell in ease, without distress, without anguish - 1) thoughts of relinquishment, 2) non-ill will, 3) non-harming, 4) perceptions of relinquishment, 5) non-ill will, 6) non-harming.
results.contentSnippet: “Chahi, bhikkhave, dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu diṭṭheva dhamme <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhaṁ</mark> viharati savighātaṁ saupāyāsaṁ sapa

results.title: Dukkhavihāra sutta - Discontented Abiding
results.description: A person endowed with the two qualities of guarding the sense doors and moderation in eating lives with discontentedness in this very life and after death, a bad destination is expected.
results.contentSnippet:
Bhojanamhi amattaññū,
indriyesu asaṁvuto;
Kāyadukkhaṁ cetodukkhaṁ,
<mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhaṁ</mark> so adhigacchati.

results.title: Dukkhanibbānasappāya sutta - Discontentment As Suitable For Realizing Nibbāna
results.description: The way of practice suitable for realizing Nibbāna is to see the six sense bases and their objects as sources of discontentment.
results.contentSnippet: Idha, bhikkhave, cakkhuṁ <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhanti</mark> passati, rūpā dukkhāti passati, cakkhuviññāṇaṁ dukkhanti passati, cakkhu

results.title: Recognition of unsatisfactoriness
results.description: Perceiving the inherent inadequacy and unreliability of conditioned existence. This recognition loosens craving and the pursuit of lasting satisfaction in what cannot endure.

results.title: Vipallāsa sutta - Inversion
results.description: The Buddha describes these four inversions of perception, thought, and view, and the four non-inversions. An uninstructed ordinary person perceives permanence in the impermanent, pleasure in the unsat
results.contentSnippet: <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhe</mark>, bhikkhave, dukkhanti nasaññāvipallāso nacittavipallāso nadiṭṭhivipallāso;

results.title: Yadanicca sutta - That Which is Impermanent
results.description: The Buddha shares a reflection on the three characteristics of impermanence, unsatisfactoriness and not-self for the five aggregates of form, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness.
results.contentSnippet: “Rūpaṁ, bhikkhave, aniccaṁ. Yadaniccaṁ taṁ <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhaṁ</mark>; yaṁ dukkhaṁ tadanattā; yadanattā taṁ ‘netaṁ mama, nesoh

results.title: Kumāraka sutta - Boys
results.description: If suffering is displeasing to you, do not commit evil deeds, whether openly or in secret.
results.contentSnippet: Atha kho bhagavā pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya sāvatthiṁ piṇḍāya pāvisi. Addasā kho bhagavā te sambahule kumārake antarā ca sāvatthiṁ antarā ca jetavanaṁ macchake bādhente. Disvāna yena

results.title: Sukha sutta - Pleasant
results.description: Seeing the vanishing nature of the experience that arises with each contact—whether felt as pleasant, painful, or as neither-painful-nor-pleasant—one becomes dispassionate towards it.
results.contentSnippet: Sukhā vedanā, <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhā</mark> vedanā, adukkhamasukhā vedanā—imā kho, bhikkhave, tisso vedanāti.

results.title: Abhinanda sutta - Delight
results.description: Whoever delights in the four great elements, delights in what is subject to suffering, and as a result, is not freed from suffering.
results.contentSnippet: “Yo, bhikkhave, pathavīdhātuṁ abhinandati, <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhaṁ</mark> so abhinandati. Yo dukkhaṁ abhinandati, aparimutto so du

results.title: Dutiya vedan sutta - Felt Experiences (Second)
results.description: The Buddha describes how to see the three felt experiences that are experienced on contact through the sense doors - pleasant, painful, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant.
results.contentSnippet: “Tisso imā, bhikkhave, vedanā. Katamā tisso? Sukhā vedanā, <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhā</mark> vedanā, adukkhamasukhā vedanā. Sukhā, bhi

results.title: Ātappakaraṇīya sutta - Vigour
results.description: The Buddha describes three cases where vigour should be applied.
results.contentSnippet: Yato kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu anuppannānaṁ pāpakānaṁ akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ anuppādāya ātappaṁ karoti, anuppannānaṁ kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ uppādāya ātappaṁ karoti, uppannānaṁ sārīrikānaṁ vedanānaṁ <mark cla

results.title: Purisa sutta - Person
results.description: When asked by King Pasenadi of Kosala about the qualities that arise within a person for their harm, suffering, and troubled living, the Buddha lists greed, aversion, and delusion, and explains their
results.contentSnippet: “Tayo kho, mahārāja, purisassa dhammā ajjhattaṁ uppajjamānā uppajjanti ahitāya <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhāya</mark> aphāsuvihārāya. Kat

results.title: Dutiya jana sutta - Gives Rise To (Second)
results.description: A deity asks the Buddha what gives rise to a person, what does he have that wanders about, what experiences cyclic existence, and from what is this person not yet freed.
results.contentSnippet:
“Taṇhā janeti purisaṁ,
cittamassa vidhāvati;
Satto saṁsāramāpādi,
<mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhā</mark> na parimuccatī”ti.

results.title: Ekantadukkha sutta - Exclusively Unpleasant
results.description: If the four great elements were exclusively unpleasant, beings would not be infatuated with them. If they were exclusively pleasurable, beings would not become disenchanted with them.
results.contentSnippet: “Pathavīdhātu ce hidaṁ, bhikkhave, ekantadukkhā abhavissa dukkhānupatitā dukkhāvakkantā anavakkantā sukhena, nayidaṁ sattā pathavīdhātuyā sārajjeyyuṁ. Yasmā ca kho, bhikkhave, pathavīdhātu sukhā sukhā

results.title: Mahādukkhakkhandha sutta - The Greater Discourse on the Mass of Suffering
results.description: The Buddha explains how to completely comprehend the gratification, drawback, and escape in the case of sensual pleasures, form, and felt experience.
results.contentSnippet: Tassa ce, bhikkhave, kulaputtassa evaṁ uṭṭhahato ghaṭato vāyamato te bhogā abhinipphajjanti. So tesaṁ bhogānaṁ ārakkhādhikaraṇaṁ <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-i

results.title: Cūḷadukkhakkhandha sutta - The Shorter Discourse on the Mass of Suffering
results.description: A lay disciple asks the Buddha why greed, aversion, and delusion still occupy and remain in his mind. The Buddha explains the importance of cultivating discernment of the gratification, drawback, and
results.contentSnippet: ‘Kiṁ pana tumhe, āvuso nigaṇṭhā, jānātha—ettakaṁ vā <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhaṁ</mark> nijjiṇṇaṁ, ettakaṁ vā dukkhaṁ nijjīretabbaṁ, et

results.title: Pariññeyya sutta - Should Be Fully Understood
results.description: The Noble Truth of suffering should be fully understood, the Noble Truth of the arising of suffering should be abandoned, the Noble Truth of the end of suffering should be personally experienced, and
results.contentSnippet: “Cattārimāni, bhikkhave, ariyasaccāni. Katamāni cattāri? <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">Dukkhaṁ</mark> ariyasaccaṁ, dukkhasamudayaṁ ariyasaccaṁ,

results.title: Dutiya sutta - Companion
results.description: A deity asks the Buddha what serves as a person‘s companion, what instructs him, and delighting in what is a mortal freed from all suffering.
results.contentSnippet:
“Kiṁsu dutiyā purisassa hoti,
Kiṁsu cenaṁ pasāsati;
Kissa cābhirato macco,
Sabba<mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhā</mark> pamuccatī”ti.

results.title: Bhidura sutta - Fragile
results.description: The body is fragile, consciousness is of a fading nature. All substrates of existence are impermanent, unsatisfactory, and subject to change.
results.contentSnippet: “Bhidurāyaṁ, bhikkhave, kāyo, viññāṇaṁ virāgadhammaṁ, sabbe upadhī aniccā <mark class="bg-yellow-100 dark:bg-yellow-900 rounded" style="padding-inline:0.05rem">dukkhā</mark> vipariṇāmadhammā”ti.
